prompt: You're on top in half guard and your opponent is on their side facing you, with their inside arm trying to get an underhook. What should you do first?
options: Secure the crossface by getting your arm under their head
Pull your trapped leg out by yanking it hard
Sit back and wait for them to make a mistake
correct_answer: Secure the crossface by getting your arm under their head
explanation: Getting the crossface flattens your opponent onto their back, taking away their underhook and their ability to turn into you — without it, they can work their guard and make passing very difficult.
coach_note: Crossface first, then worry about your leg — control their head and the body follows.

prompt: You're flat on your back in side control. Opponent has your near arm pinned. What's your first move?
options: Frame your forearm at their hip and your other hand at their neck/shoulder
Grab their belt and pull them down
Try to bench press them off your chest
correct_answer: Frame your forearm at their hip and your other hand at their neck/shoulder
explanation: Frames create distance and space, which is the foundation of every escape. Without frames, you have no room to move.
coach_note: Think of frames as your seatbelt — put them on first, before anything else.

prompt: You've framed at the hip and neck in side control. What do you do next to recover guard?
options: Shrimp your hips away to create space for your knee to come in
Immediately try to roll them over
Reach for a collar grip
correct_answer: Shrimp your hips away to create space for your knee to come in
explanation: Shrimping moves your hips away from the opponent, creating the gap needed to insert your knee and recover half or full guard.
coach_note: Your hips do the work — not your arms. Shrimp hard.

prompt: You're in side control bottom and your frames are getting crushed flat. What should you do immediately?
options: Re-establish frames before they collapse completely
Bridge as hard as you can
Grab their arm and go for a submission
correct_answer: Re-establish frames before they collapse completely
explanation: Once your frames are gone, you lose the ability to create space. Restoring frames is the priority before attempting any escape.
coach_note: Don't wait until your frames are totally flat — reset them early.

prompt: Opponent is in side control and starting to creep toward your head to take mount. What do you do?
options: Hip escape toward them and insert your knee as a barrier
Push their head with both hands
Go completely flat to slow them down
correct_answer: Hip escape toward them and insert your knee as a barrier
explanation: Moving your hips toward them and getting a knee in disrupts the mount transition and can recover guard before they complete the move.
coach_note: React early — once they're in mount it's a whole new problem.

prompt: You're in side control bottom and you feel the opponent's weight shift. You have space on one side. What's the best escape attempt right now?
options: Shrimp into the space and recover your guard
Immediately sit up
Stay still and wait for more space
correct_answer: Shrimp into the space and recover your guard
explanation: Weight shifts create momentary space — you must exploit them immediately by shrimping to recover guard before they settle back.
coach_note: Escapes happen in windows. Move when space appears.

prompt: You're pinned in side control and your opponent is very heavy. You can't shrimp. What do you try?
options: Bridge explosively into them to disrupt their base
Go limp and conserve energy
Try to grab their leg from underneath
correct_answer: Bridge explosively into them to disrupt their base
explanation: Bridging into the opponent shifts their weight and can create enough space to shrimp or roll them off you.
coach_note: Bridge first to create space, shrimp second to use it.

prompt: You bridge hard into your opponent in side control. They base out. What do you do at that exact moment?
options: Shrimp away and insert your knee while their base is split
Keep bridging harder
Grab their collar for a choke
correct_answer: Shrimp away and insert your knee while their base is split
explanation: The bridge disrupts their base temporarily — that's your cue to immediately shrimp and recover guard before they resettle.
coach_note: Bridge and shrimp are a combo, not two separate ideas.

prompt: You're in side control bottom. Opponent has their chest heavy on yours and their far arm posted on the ground. What do you do?
options: Frame at the hip with your bottom arm and bridge into them toward their posted arm side
Push their face away
Go to turtle by turning away
correct_answer: Frame at the hip with your bottom arm and bridge into them toward their posted arm side
explanation: Bridging toward their posted arm forces them to fall in that direction, giving you the chance to roll them or create space.
coach_note: Bridge toward where they're already leaning — never into their strong base.

prompt: You're in side control and you manage to turn into your opponent to face them. What are you trying to do next?
options: Shoot a single leg or get your inside leg between you to recover guard
Go for a headlock
Push them away with straight arms
correct_answer: Shoot a single leg or get your inside leg between you to recover guard
explanation: Turning in gives you access to their legs for a single leg takedown attempt or allows you to wedge your knee between you to recover guard.
coach_note: Turning in is only useful if you do something with it — commit to the shot or the knee.

prompt: You're in side control bottom and opponent is posting on your hip with their knee. Your frames are in place. What do you do?
options: Shrimp away from the posted knee and try to recover half guard
Try to remove their knee with your hand
Do nothing and wait for them to move
correct_answer: Shrimp away from the posted knee and try to recover half guard
explanation: Their posted knee creates a reference point — shrimping away from it creates space to insert your leg and at least recover half guard.
coach_note: Half guard is a step toward full guard — take it when you can.

prompt: Opponent is in side control and reaching under your head to lock in a head-and-arm position. What do you do right now?
options: Tuck your chin and frame to prevent them from locking up the position
Reach over their back with both arms
Try to roll away from them immediately
correct_answer: Tuck your chin and frame to prevent them from locking up the position
explanation: Head-and-arm control is dangerous and limits your escapes — preventing the grip before it's locked in is far easier than escaping it after.
coach_note: Defense starts before they complete the grip, not after.

prompt: You're in side control bottom and you feel your near arm getting trapped across your body. What's the danger?
options: It exposes you to Kimura or keeps you flat and unable to escape
You could roll them easily from here
Nothing — it's a neutral position
correct_answer: It exposes you to Kimura or keeps you flat and unable to escape
explanation: A trapped near arm kills your frames and exposes you to shoulder submissions. Keep your near arm free and framing whenever possible.
coach_note: Protect your near arm like it's your most valuable escape tool — because it is.

prompt: You're in side control bottom. You've been trying to escape for a while and you're getting tired. What's the smart move?
options: Prioritize survival frames and breathe, then look for the right moment to escape
Keep fighting constantly to show effort
Give up position and let them take mount to reset
correct_answer: Prioritize survival frames and breathe, then look for the right moment to escape
explanation: Burning out makes everything worse. Frames let you survive while you recover breath and wait for an opportunity to escape.
coach_note: Survival mode is not giving up — it's being smart.

prompt: You're in side control bottom and have created space with a shrimp. Your opponent is adjusting. What do you do with the space?
options: Shoot your bottom knee in toward their hip to recover guard or half guard
Sit up and grab their collar
Roll away from them to turtle
correct_answer: Shoot your bottom knee in toward their hip to recover guard or half guard
explanation: The space created by shrimping is only useful if you immediately insert your knee — that's the movement that recovers guard.
coach_note: Shrimp and knee in are one fluid motion, not two separate steps.

prompt: Opponent in side control starts to pressure your neck with their forearm. What do you do?
options: Tuck your chin down and push their elbow to clear the pressure
Turn away from them to escape
Grab their wrist with both hands
correct_answer: Tuck your chin down and push their elbow to clear the pressure
explanation: Tucking your chin protects your neck from choke attempts, and pushing the elbow helps clear their arm so you can restore your frame.
coach_note: Chin down is a reflex you need to build. Drill it until it's automatic.

prompt: You've recovered half guard from side control. Opponent is trying to pass again. What's your job right now?
options: Work to recover full guard or improve your half guard position
Try to immediately sweep them
Go flat and rest
correct_answer: Work to recover full guard or improve your half guard position
explanation: Half guard is a step forward but not safe — continue working to recover full guard or get to a better half guard position.
coach_note: Never settle in half guard — keep building toward full guard.

prompt: You're in side control bottom and the opponent has their knee next to your hip. You want to turn to turtle. Which direction do you turn?
options: Toward them, getting to all fours facing them
Away from them, rolling to your belly
Straight up, trying to sit up
correct_answer: Toward them, getting to all fours facing them
explanation: Turning toward the opponent gets you to a defensive turtle position facing them, which is safer than turning away and exposing your back.
coach_note: Turning away gives up your back — turn in to face them whenever possible.

prompt: You're in turtle position under your opponent's side control. They are trying to flatten you out. What do you do?
options: Stay tight, widen your base, and protect your neck with your elbows
Roll immediately to try an escape
Flatten yourself out to reduce pressure
correct_answer: Stay tight, widen your base, and protect your neck with your elbows
explanation: A tight turtle with a wide base is hard to flatten. Keeping your elbows in protects your neck from chokes while you look for your next move.
coach_note: Turtle is a rest stop, not a destination — survive first, then plan your next move.

prompt: You're in side control bottom and the opponent is very low, hugging your body tightly. You can barely move. What do you try?
options: Create a small frame at the hip with your elbow and use small shrimp movements
Try to roll them with a big explosive bridge
Grab their leg with both hands
correct_answer: Create a small frame at the hip with your elbow and use small shrimp movements
explanation: When space is minimal, small incremental movements and micro-frames are more effective than big explosive attempts that you don't have room to complete.
coach_note: In tight situations, small and consistent beats big and explosive.

prompt: Your opponent in side control has your far arm and is setting up a far-side armbar. What do you do?
options: Pull your arm back close to your body and frame to protect it
Let them take the arm and try to roll out
Turn away from them quickly
correct_answer: Pull your arm back close to your body and frame to protect it
explanation: Keeping your arms close to your body denies their grip on your far arm. An arm close to you is far harder to finish than one that's isolated.
coach_note: Straight arms away from your body get caught. Keep them bent and close.

prompt: You're in side control bottom and you push their hip with your bottom arm but your elbow is flaring wide. What's the problem?
options: A wide elbow creates a lever for them to collapse your frame and go tighter
A wide elbow gives you more pushing power
There's no problem — a wider base is always better
correct_answer: A wide elbow creates a lever for them to collapse your frame and go tighter
explanation: When your elbow flares out, the opponent can drive into it and fold your arm, collapsing your frame entirely. Keep elbows in line with your wrist.
coach_note: Frames are only strong when your elbow points down, not out.

prompt: You're in side control bottom. Opponent is kneeling upright, not much weight on you. What should you do?
options: Sit up into them and try to get to your knees or get underhook
Stay flat and continue trying to shrimp
Do nothing since they're not heavy on you
correct_answer: Sit up into them and try to get to your knees or get underhook
explanation: When the opponent is upright with less weight on you, sitting up to fight for an underhook or go to your knees is a great opportunity to escape.
coach_note: Light pressure is your cue to sit up and be active.

prompt: You try to shrimp and your opponent follows your hips and stays tight. What do you do?
options: Bridge to create space again, then immediately shrimp
Keep shrimping in the same direction
Give up on escaping and just survive
correct_answer: Bridge to create space again, then immediately shrimp
explanation: If shrimping alone didn't work, you need to use a bridge to temporarily displace them and create fresh space before trying again.
coach_note: Bridge and shrimp in combination is much harder to follow than either alone.

prompt: You're in side control bottom and you get your knee in between you and your opponent. They start to push the knee down. What do you do?
options: Fight to keep the knee up and pull guard or go to half guard
Remove the knee and try a different approach
Straighten your leg to stop them
correct_answer: Fight to keep the knee up and pull guard or go to half guard
explanation: Your inside knee is your best tool for recovering guard — fight hard to keep it elevated and use it to pull into guard before they flatten it out.
coach_note: Your knee in = their pass failing. Fight for that position every time.

prompt: You're in side control bottom and your opponent shifts all their weight toward your head. Your hips have space. What do you do?
options: Immediately shrimp your hips away and insert your knee to recover guard
Sit up toward the space
Bridge toward their head
correct_answer: Immediately shrimp your hips away and insert your knee to recover guard
explanation: When the opponent shifts toward your head, your hips are freed — use that instant to shrimp and insert your knee before they react.
coach_note: Hip space is the green light to shrimp. Move without hesitation.

prompt: You're in side control bottom and you see the opponent posting one hand far from their body. What does this tell you?
options: Their base is extended — this is a good time to bridge toward that side
They're about to submit you
You should go to turtle away from that side
correct_answer: Their base is extended — this is a good time to bridge toward that side
explanation: A wide post means their weight is extended in that direction. Bridging toward the posted arm gives you a chance to displace them or roll them.
coach_note: Always bridge into weakness, not into strength.

prompt: You're in side control and the opponent is trying to take mount by stepping over your stomach. What do you do right now?
options: Block their knee with your hand and shrimp away to prevent mount
Let them mount and try to escape from there
Grab their foot to try a leg lock
correct_answer: Block their knee with your hand and shrimp away to prevent mount
explanation: Blocking the stepping knee early stops the mount transition before it completes. Combined with a shrimp, you can deny mount and maintain side control bottom.
coach_note: Stop the knee, not the leg. That's where to intercept the move.

prompt: You're flat in side control and your opponent is settling their hips low. You want to bridge. Which way do you bridge?
options: Into them toward their near hip to displace their weight
Away from them toward the floor
Straight up toward the ceiling
correct_answer: Into them toward their near hip to displace their weight
explanation: Bridging into them disrupts their base and creates space. Bridging away or straight up is ineffective and can make things worse.
coach_note: Bridge into them, not away. You want to move them, not yourself.

prompt: You're in side control bottom and you try to turn in to face your opponent. They base out hard and stop you. What do you do?
options: Use that moment of resistance to shrimp the other way and recover guard
Keep pushing into them harder
Go flat and reset
correct_answer: Use that moment of resistance to shrimp the other way and recover guard
explanation: When they resist your turn-in, their energy is committed in one direction — immediately reverse and shrimp away to use their force against them.
coach_note: When they push back, go the other way. Reaction creates opportunity.

prompt: You're in side control bottom. Opponent has an underhook under your near arm. What are they setting up?
options: A tighter control that could lead to north-south or a submission — you should frame and prevent it deepening
A guard recovery for you
A sweep attempt
correct_answer: A tighter control that could lead to north-south or a submission — you should frame and prevent it deepening
explanation: An underhook under your near arm gives the opponent strong control and options to move to north-south or set up submissions. Frame and work to clear it.
coach_note: Don't give up the underhook without a fight — it's their control tool.

prompt: You've been in side control for a long time. You're tiring and the opponent is fresh. What's your escape priority?
options: Make one good explosive attempt using a bridge-shrimp combo when they shift weight
Try many different escapes rapidly to find one that works
Stay completely still to conserve energy
correct_answer: Make one good explosive attempt using a bridge-shrimp combo when they shift weight
explanation: When tired, wasting energy on failed attempts is dangerous. Wait for a clear opportunity and commit fully to one well-timed explosive escape.
coach_note: One committed attempt beats ten sloppy ones every time.

prompt: You're in side control bottom and get a deep underhook on your opponent. What do you do with it?
options: Use the underhook to turn into them, come to your knees, or work a reversal
Pull them down to keep them close
Reach for their legs with the underhook arm
correct_answer: Use the underhook to turn into them, come to your knees, or work a reversal
explanation: A deep underhook from the bottom gives you the leverage to turn in, come to your knees, or force a scramble — all are better than your current position.
coach_note: Underhook = your ticket to get back in the fight. Use it aggressively.

prompt: You're in side control bottom. The opponent is trying to flatten your top arm across your neck. What do you do?
options: Frame their shoulder away with your top arm and tuck your chin to protect the neck
Grab their wrist with your bottom hand
Bridge into them immediately
correct_answer: Frame their shoulder away with your top arm and tuck your chin to protect the neck
explanation: Keeping your top arm active as a frame and tucking your chin prevents them from locking in a choke or further restricting your movement.
coach_note: Chin down, arm framing — those two things solve most neck threats.

prompt: You've turned in and your face is now toward the opponent in side control. Their legs are right there. What can you try?
options: Shoot for a single leg to try to get back to your feet
Go for an ankle lock
Stay there and try to get back to guard
correct_answer: Shoot for a single leg to try to get back to your feet
explanation: Turning in puts you in position to shoot a single leg, which can get you back to standing or at least into a scramble where you can recover position.
coach_note: Turning in is a setup for the single leg shot — commit to it.

prompt: You're in side control bottom and your opponent's hips are far from you. What does that tell you?
options: There's space between you — try to wedge your knee in and recover guard
They are about to pass further
You should bridge now
correct_answer: There's space between you — try to wedge your knee in and recover guard
explanation: Distance between hips means space to insert your knee. This is the ideal moment to shoot your knee in and recover guard.
coach_note: Far hips = space to insert your knee. Don't waste that opportunity.

prompt: You're in side control bottom and your arm accidentally gets extended and posted on the mat. What should you do immediately?
options: Pull your arm back to your body before they can isolate it for a submission
Push off the mat to try to create space
Keep the arm posted for base
correct_answer: Pull your arm back to your body before they can isolate it for a submission
explanation: A posted arm away from your body is vulnerable to Kimura, armbar, or wrist lock. Get it back to safety before they notice the opportunity.
coach_note: Straight arms away from your body are a gift to your opponent. Keep them close.

prompt: Opponent is in side control and you can feel them step their knee over to mount. You blocked it once but they keep trying. What's your job?
options: Keep blocking the knee with your hand and keep shrimping away to deny the space they need
Accept mount and try to escape from there
Bridge into them each time they try
correct_answer: Keep blocking the knee with your hand and keep shrimping away to deny the space they need
explanation: Consistently blocking the knee and removing the space they need is how you stop a persistent mount transition attempt. Don't stop until the threat is gone.
coach_note: Block it every time. They only need to succeed once — you need to stop it every time.

prompt: You're in side control bottom. Your opponent is very light and you can bridge them easily. What do you do after the bridge?
options: Immediately shrimp away and recover guard while they're mid-air or off-balance
Keep bridging until they fall off completely
Pin them down on the other side
correct_answer: Immediately shrimp away and recover guard while they're mid-air or off-balance
explanation: The bridge creates momentary off-balance — the shrimp uses that moment to recover guard. The bridge alone won't finish the escape.
coach_note: Bridge gets them up, shrimp gets you out. Always combine them.

prompt: You're in side control bottom and feel completely stuck. You've tried shrimping and bridging. What's your basic survival job right now?
options: Maintain frames at hip and neck, breathe, and stay patient for an opportunity
Go completely flat and give up escaping for now
Start grabbing random limbs to create chaos
correct_answer: Maintain frames at hip and neck, breathe, and stay patient for an opportunity
explanation: When escapes aren't working, the priority is survival — frames keep you safe and buy time until the opponent makes a mistake or shifts weight.
coach_note: Patience is a skill. Surviving well is winning until you can escape.

prompt: You just got reversed from side control to turtle. Your opponent is behind you on top. What do you protect first?
options: Your neck, by keeping your chin down and elbows tucked to prevent the choke
Your legs, pulling them in tight
Your arms, extending them for base
correct_answer: Your neck, by keeping your chin down and elbows tucked to prevent the choke
explanation: In turtle, the biggest danger is getting choked or having your neck attacked. Chin down and elbows tight to the body is your first line of defense.
coach_note: Turtle survival is all about protecting your neck first.

prompt: You're in mount top — your opponent suddenly bucks their hips hard and rolls to one side. What do you do?
options: Post your hand out to the mat and base wide to stay on top
Squeeze your knees tight and freeze
Jump off and take side control voluntarily
correct_answer: Post your hand out to the mat and base wide to stay on top
explanation: Posting your hand gives you a wide base so you don't get rolled off. Freezing with just your knees lets their bridge knock you over.
coach_note: Think of your posted arm as a kickstand — it stops the roll.

prompt: You're in mount top — opponent bridges hard and tries to roll you over their right shoulder. What is your first move?
options: Post your left hand out and sprawl your hips to the right
Grab their belt and hold tight
Flatten out and squeeze your legs
correct_answer: Post your left hand out and sprawl your hips to the right
explanation: Posting opposite the direction of the bridge and shifting your hips kills their momentum before the roll completes.
coach_note: Move your hips the same way they're trying to roll you — it takes away their leverage.

prompt: You're in mount top — opponent does a big upa bridge and you feel yourself tipping. What should you do with your arm?
options: Post the arm on the side you're being rolled toward
Pull both arms in tight to your body
Immediately try to take their back
correct_answer: Post the arm on the side you're being rolled toward
explanation: Posting on the side of the roll creates a tripod that stops you from being tipped over. Both arms pulled in gives you no base at all.

prompt: You're in mount top — opponent starts bringing their knee up toward your butt to begin an elbow-knee escape. What do you do?
options: Slide your knee back to block their knee from coming through
Lift your hips to give them room
Switch immediately to side control
correct_answer: Slide your knee back to block their knee from coming through
explanation: Closing the space between your knee and their hip prevents them from creating the frame needed for the elbow-knee escape.
coach_note: Your knees are your gatekeepers — keep them tight to their hips.

prompt: You're in mount top — opponent's knee is already halfway through toward your hip. What do you do now?
options: Shoot your hips forward and try to flatten them out
Give up mount and go to half guard top
Push their knee down with your hand
correct_answer: Shoot your hips forward and try to flatten them out
explanation: Driving your hips forward takes away the space they need to complete the elbow-knee escape and flattens their frame.
coach_note: Hip pressure forward is your best tool when they're already halfway through.

prompt: You're in mount top — opponent is trying to elbow-knee escape and has created a frame with their elbow and knee. What should you do with your weight?
options: Sink your hips down and forward, keeping weight heavy
Rise up onto your knees to free the space
Lean back to avoid being framed
correct_answer: Sink your hips down and forward, keeping weight heavy
explanation: Heavy hip pressure collapses their frame and makes it hard for them to push and create the room they need to escape.

prompt: You're in mount top — you want to advance to high mount. Where do you move your knees?
options: Walk your knees up toward their armpits
Keep your knees at their hips and sit up tall
Drop your knees down toward their legs
correct_answer: Walk your knees up toward their armpits
explanation: Moving your knees toward the armpits puts you in high mount, which limits their arms and sets up chokes and armbars much more easily.
coach_note: Walk up slowly — big jumps lose the position.

prompt: You're moving to high mount — opponent pushes up on your thigh to stop you from climbing. What do you do?
options: Trap that arm and use it to pull yourself higher
Stop moving and stay in regular mount
Reach for a choke immediately
correct_answer: Trap that arm and use it to pull yourself higher
explanation: When they push on your thigh, they've straightened that arm — trap it and use it as a handle to climb into high mount while controlling the limb.
coach_note: Their defense becomes your ladder to climb higher.

prompt: You're in high mount — opponent tucks both arms tight to their chest. What should you do?
options: Work to slide your knees over their arms to trap them
Immediately go back to regular mount
Stand up and jump to an armbar
correct_answer: Work to slide your knees over their arms to trap them
explanation: Sliding your knees over their tucked arms pins them and opens up chokes from the top like the cross choke.

prompt: You're in mount top — you want to set up the cross-face. Where do you place your arm?
options: Drive your shoulder into their jaw and reach under their far shoulder
Place your hand flat on their forehead
Hook your arm under their near arm
correct_answer: Drive your shoulder into their jaw and reach under their far shoulder
explanation: The cross-face uses your shoulder pressure on their chin/jaw and your arm under their far shoulder to rotate their head away and flatten them out.
coach_note: Shoulder pressure turns their head, making bridging much harder.

prompt: You have a cross-face in mount — opponent is still turning into you. What do you do with the cross-face pressure?
options: Increase shoulder pressure to force their head the other way
Release the cross-face and grab their collar
Move to the other side
correct_answer: Increase shoulder pressure to force their head the other way
explanation: The cross-face works by turning the head — if they're turning in, you need more pressure to rotate their head away and kill their bridge.

prompt: You're in mount top — you want to set up the arm triangle. Which arm position do you need from your opponent?
options: One arm up by their head and one arm trapped across their body
Both arms out flat on the mat
Both arms bent tight to their chest
correct_answer: One arm up by their head and one arm trapped across their body
explanation: The arm triangle requires their arm to be trapped across their own neck so your arm and their arm create the choking mechanism together.
coach_note: Think of it as making them choke themselves with their own arm.

prompt: You're setting up an arm triangle from mount — you've trapped their arm across their neck. What do you do next?
options: Bring your head down to the mat on the far side and start to move to side control
Sit up and squeeze from mount
Post up and throw your leg over for an armbar
correct_answer: Bring your head down to the mat on the far side and start to move to side control
explanation: To finish the arm triangle you need to consolidate by moving your head to the mat and transitioning to side control where you can lock your hands and squeeze.

prompt: You're in mount top — you have the arm triangle locked but opponent is blocking your transition to side control. What do you do?
options: Keep squeezing and use your legs to drive through their block
Release the arm triangle and go back to neutral mount
Try to finish the choke from mount without moving
correct_answer: Keep squeezing and use your legs to drive through their block
explanation: Maintaining the choke while using hip and leg pressure to push through the block keeps them under pressure and usually forces the finish or the transition.
coach_note: Don't let go — the squeeze itself may get the tap.

prompt: You're in mount top — you want to hit an armbar. You've isolated their arm. What do you do with your legs?
options: Swing your far leg over their face and sit back, pinching your knees together
Keep both legs in mount and pull the arm up
Stand up and step over their body
correct_answer: Swing your far leg over their face and sit back, pinching your knees together
explanation: Swinging your leg over their face and sitting back with knees pinched is the fundamental armbar finish from mount — it controls the arm and applies the extension.
coach_note: Pinch those knees — if your knees are open, they can pull the arm out.

prompt: You're attempting an armbar from mount — opponent tucks their elbow to escape as you swing your leg over. What do you do?
options: Grab their wrist tightly and keep your leg pressure on their head
Abandon the armbar and reset to mount
Switch immediately to the other arm
correct_answer: Grab their wrist tightly and keep your leg pressure on their head
explanation: Controlling their wrist prevents the tuck escape, and leg pressure on their head keeps them from sitting up to relieve the armbar.

prompt: You're going for an armbar from mount and opponent is turning into you before you can swing your leg over. What should you do?
options: Post your outside arm and base out to stop the roll, then reset
Continue swinging your leg regardless
Let them turn and take the back
correct_answer: Post your outside arm and base out to stop the roll, then reset
explanation: If they're turning before you swing your leg over, you haven't committed yet — base out, stop the roll, and look for a better moment.
coach_note: Don't commit to the armbar if they're already turning — timing matters.

prompt: You're in mount top — opponent has their arms flat out to the sides and is not defending. What is the best opportunity here?
options: Slide into high mount and attack a choke or armbar
Stay in low mount and wait
Drop to side control
correct_answer: Slide into high mount and attack a choke or armbar
explanation: Arms flat to the sides is an invitation — advance to high mount immediately to exploit the lack of defense before they remember to bring their arms in.
coach_note: Take what they give you. Arms flat means high mount time.

prompt: You're in mount top — you want to apply grapevine hooks. What do you do with your feet?
options: Hook your feet inside their legs and straighten your body to stretch them out
Cross your feet behind their knees
Hook your heels on the outside of their thighs
correct_answer: Hook your feet inside their legs and straighten your body to stretch them out
explanation: Grapevines use your feet hooked inside their legs — straightening your body flattens them out and prevents them from getting their knees up to bridge or escape.

prompt: You have grapevine hooks in mount — opponent is trying to bend their knees to create a bridge. What do you do?
options: Squeeze and extend your grapevines to keep their legs flat
Release the grapevines to reposition
Sit up to change your weight distribution
correct_answer: Squeeze and extend your grapevines to keep their legs flat
explanation: Extending your body through the grapevines prevents them from bending their knees, which removes the ability to generate upward force for a bridge.
coach_note: Grapevines work by extension — squeeze and stretch.

prompt: You're in mount top — opponent has one arm extended, posting on your hip. What should you do?
options: Trap that wrist against their body and attack the isolated arm
Push their arm away and wait
Jump to the other side for a choke
correct_answer: Trap that wrist against their body and attack the isolated arm
explanation: An extended arm is a gift — trap the wrist, control the elbow, and set up an armbar or kimura before they realize the mistake.
coach_note: Straight arm = danger for them. React fast.

prompt: You're in mount top — opponent grabs your collar with both hands trying to push you away. What is your best response?
options: Bring your elbows down inside their arms to break the frame
Sit back to relieve the pressure
Stand up on your feet
correct_answer: Bring your elbows down inside their arms to break the frame
explanation: Driving your elbows to the inside of their arms collapses their push frame and returns your chest pressure onto them.

prompt: You're in mount top — opponent manages to get half guard and traps one of your legs. What do you do?
options: Work to free your trapped leg and return to full mount
Accept half guard top and fight from there
Immediately try a choke from half mount
correct_answer: Work to free your trapped leg and return to full mount
explanation: Half guard is an inferior position compared to full mount — always try to recover your leg and re-establish full mount before your opponent improves their position.
coach_note: Never settle for losing ground. Fight to get mount back.

prompt: You're in mount top — your hips are sitting too high and opponent starts to bench press you upward. What do you do?
options: Drop your hips down and forward to reconnect heavy pressure
Grab their wrists to stop the push
Try to move to high mount before they finish the push
correct_answer: Drop your hips down and forward to reconnect heavy pressure
explanation: High hips give them room to push — dropping your hips forward and down kills the frame and reapplies your weight where they can't generate force.
coach_note: Low hips = control. High hips = their opportunity.

prompt: You're in mount top — opponent turns to their side and tries to get to their knees. What do you do?
options: Take their back by putting in your hooks as they turn
Try to force them flat by pushing on their shoulder
Jump off to the side and take side control
correct_answer: Take their back by putting in your hooks as they turn
explanation: When they turn to get to their knees, they're giving you their back — slide your hooks in and take back control, which is an even better position.
coach_note: Turning into you from mount? That's a back take in progress.

prompt: You're in mount top — opponent is completely flat and not moving. What should you be doing?
options: Use the stillness to advance to high mount and set up a submission
Wait for them to move before reacting
Stay in low mount to conserve energy
correct_answer: Use the stillness to advance to high mount and set up a submission
explanation: A still opponent is an opportunity — advance your position and set up attacks while they aren't defending. Waiting lets them recover and plan.

prompt: You're in mount top — you try a choke but it's not tight. Opponent starts to roll. What do you do?
options: Abandon the loose choke, base out with your arm, and reset
Force the choke anyway
Roll with them and give up top position
correct_answer: Abandon the loose choke, base out with your arm, and reset
explanation: A loose choke is not going to finish — forcing it costs you mount. Base out, stop the roll, and look for a better setup.
coach_note: If the submission isn't tight, don't commit to it.

prompt: You're in mount top — opponent shrimps hard and gets their knee inside. What do you do immediately?
options: Drive your hip forward and try to pin their knee to the mat
Hop over their knee to the other side
Give up mount and take side control
correct_answer: Drive your hip forward and try to pin their knee to the mat
explanation: Driving your hip forward collapses the space created by the shrimp and can trap their knee before they complete the escape.
coach_note: React fast — once the knee is fully through, mount is gone.

prompt: You're in mount top — opponent wraps their arms around your back in a bear hug, limiting your movement. What do you do?
options: Post on your hands, create space, and work to break the grip
Stay still and accept the position
Immediately abandon mount
correct_answer: Post on your hands, create space, and work to break the grip
explanation: Posting on your hands raises your chest to create space, which breaks the bear hug grip and lets you regain control of the position.

prompt: You're in mount top — you want to maintain position but you feel yourself getting tired. What is the most important thing to do?
options: Keep your hips heavy and low to minimize the effort needed to hold mount
Squeeze your legs harder to tire them out faster
Try to finish quickly with any submission you can find
correct_answer: Keep your hips heavy and low to minimize the effort needed to hold mount
explanation: Low heavy hips do the work of maintaining mount through gravity and weight, not muscle — this conserves energy while keeping control.
coach_note: Let your weight do the work, not your muscles.

prompt: You're in high mount — opponent finally gets their hands free and tries to push you off. What do you do?
options: Use their pushing arms to attack a choke or armbar
Scoot back to low mount
Grab their wrists and hold them down
correct_answer: Use their pushing arms to attack a choke or armbar
explanation: Pushing arms are extended and accessible — trap the wrist, isolate the elbow, and attack the armbar or use the frame to set up a choke.
coach_note: In high mount, their hands going up is opportunity, not a problem.

prompt: You're in mount top — opponent bucks and you feel completely off balance. What is your immediate priority?
options: Survive the scramble and try to land in a dominant position
Force yourself back to mount regardless
Hold on and hope they stop bucking
correct_answer: Survive the scramble and try to land in a dominant position
explanation: Sometimes mount is lost in a scramble — the priority is not to panic but to use good instincts to land in side control, back mount, or another dominant spot.

prompt: You're in mount top — opponent has their chin tucked tight to their chest, blocking your choke attempts. What do you do?
options: Use your arm or shoulder to apply cross-face pressure and force the chin up
Give up the choke and only look for armbars
Grab their collar and pull upward
correct_answer: Use your arm or shoulder to apply cross-face pressure and force the chin up
explanation: Cross-face shoulder pressure on the jaw forces the head to rotate away, exposing the neck and making chokes accessible again.
coach_note: Tucked chin blocks chokes — cross-face opens the neck.

prompt: You're in mount top — opponent pushes up on your hips strongly. What do you do with your weight?
options: Lean your chest forward and down to counter their push
Sit upright and let them push
Move to side control before they can push you off
correct_answer: Lean your chest forward and down to counter their push
explanation: Leaning forward shifts your center of gravity forward, making their upward push ineffective and increasing chest pressure on them.

prompt: You're in mount top — opponent is tiny and very flexible, making bridging very effective. What is your main focus?
options: Apply grapevines and use heavy chest pressure to limit their movement
Keep trying to hold mount without any changes
Move to side control where they can't bridge
correct_answer: Apply grapevines and use heavy chest pressure to limit their movement
explanation: Grapevines limit the hip mobility needed for a strong bridge, and chest pressure adds weight that is harder to lift against a flexible opponent.
coach_note: Grapevines are your answer to explosive bridgers.

prompt: You're in mount top — you've set up the armbar but as you sit back, opponent clasps their hands together to defend. What do you do?
options: Keep squeezing your knees and try to break their grip by extending your hips
Let go and go back to mount
Switch to an arm triangle instead
correct_answer: Keep squeezing your knees and try to break their grip by extending your hips
explanation: Squeezing your knees isolates the arm, and hip extension applies leverage to break their grip — this is the basic armbar defense break for white belt level.
coach_note: Knees tight first, then extend. Sequence matters.

prompt: You're in mount top — you try to advance to high mount but opponent immediately bridges hard when you move. What should you do?
options: Post out and base wide, then try again when they settle
Stay in low mount and never try to advance
Keep trying to advance during the bridge
correct_answer: Post out and base wide, then try again when they settle
explanation: A bridge during your advance can roll you — base out first, stabilize, wait for them to settle and tire, then try the advance again.

prompt: You're in mount top — opponent grabs your sleeve with both hands and tries to pull your arm across their body. What do you do?
options: Pull your elbow back and keep it tight to your body
Let them pull and see what happens
Give up the arm and try a choke with the other arm
correct_answer: Pull your elbow back and keep it tight to your body
explanation: Keeping your elbow in tight prevents them from isolating your arm for an armbar or upa escape — never let them control your arm in mount.
coach_note: Elbows in tight from mount top. Always.

prompt: You're in mount top — opponent is very defensive and just holding on. You've been in mount for 30 seconds. What is the right approach?
options: Patiently advance position and look for openings rather than forcing submissions
Try any submission you can reach right now
Drop to side control for a better angle
correct_answer: Patiently advance position and look for openings rather than forcing submissions
explanation: Forcing submissions from mount leads to scrambles that lose position — advance methodically and wait for their defense to break before committing to an attack.
coach_note: Mount is a patient game. Improve position first, submit second.

prompt: You're in mount top — you feel opponent about to explode into a bridge. What can you do before they initiate?
options: Preemptively drop your hips and apply cross-face to disrupt the timing
Wait to react until after they bridge
Jump to high mount before they bridge
correct_answer: Preemptively drop your hips and apply cross-face to disrupt the timing
explanation: Feeling the tension and acting first — dropping hips and applying cross-face — disrupts their setup before they generate the power for a full bridge.
coach_note: The best defense is feeling it coming before it happens.

prompt: You're flat on your back under mount. Your opponent's arm is low and close to your body. What's your first move toward an upa escape?
options: Trap their wrist and the same-side ankle, then bridge
Push their chest with both hands
Tuck your chin and wait for them to move
correct_answer: Trap their wrist and the same-side ankle, then bridge
explanation: The upa requires you to trap the arm and leg on the same side so the opponent cannot post out when you bridge. Pushing the chest does nothing to prevent them from posting.
coach_note: Wrist first, then ankle — nail the sequence.

prompt: You try to bridge for the upa but your opponent easily posts their free hand on the mat and stays on top. What went wrong?
options: You didn't trap their near leg before bridging
You bridged too high
You forgot to turn your head
correct_answer: You didn't trap their near leg before bridging
explanation: If the leg is free, the opponent can post it out and easily resist the bridge. Trapping the leg removes their base on that side.
coach_note: No leg trap = no upa. Always check both traps.

prompt: You're under mount and you want to do the elbow-knee escape. Which direction do you push your elbow?
options: Push your elbow toward your same-side knee to create a frame and recover guard
Push your elbow straight up toward the ceiling
Push your elbow across your body toward the opposite hip
correct_answer: Push your elbow toward your same-side knee to create a frame and recover guard
explanation: The elbow-knee escape works by framing your elbow to your knee, creating a gap so you can slide your knee inside and recover half guard or full guard.
coach_note: Elbow and knee meet in the middle — think about closing that gap.

prompt: You're under mount. Your opponent sits up high with their knees near your armpits. You try an elbow-knee escape but can't get your knee inside. What should you do instead?
options: Switch to the upa — trap the arm and leg and bridge
Try to push their knee with your hands
Go flat and wait for them to come down
correct_answer: Switch to the upa — trap the arm and leg and bridge
explanation: High mount makes the elbow-knee escape very difficult because there's no space for your knee. The upa is often more accessible from high mount because their weight is further forward.
coach_note: High mount = think upa first.

prompt: You're under mount and your opponent grabs your collar with both hands to choke you. What's the best response?
options: Trap one arm and leg immediately and upa while they're focused on the choke grip
Grab both their wrists and push their arms away
Curl up to protect your neck
correct_answer: Trap one arm and leg immediately and upa while they're focused on the choke grip
explanation: When your opponent commits both hands to a choke, their posture is compromised and they can't post as easily. This is the perfect moment to trap and bridge.
coach_note: Their attack is your escape window.

prompt: You're under mount and you bridge hard but your opponent doesn't roll. You land back flat. What do you do next?
options: Immediately shoot your knee inside for the elbow-knee escape before they resecure
Bridge again right away in the same direction
Go limp and conserve energy
correct_answer: Immediately shoot your knee inside for the elbow-knee escape before they resecure
explanation: After a failed upa your opponent's weight shifts and there's a brief moment of instability — use it to switch to the elbow-knee escape before they resettle.
coach_note: Failed upa creates a small window. Don't waste it.

prompt: You're under mount. You feel your opponent starting to slide their arm under your neck for a choke. What do you do?
options: Tuck your chin, turn into them, and start your elbow-knee escape
Grab their arm with both hands and pull it away
Bridge immediately even without trapping
correct_answer: Tuck your chin, turn into them, and start your elbow-knee escape
explanation: Tucking your chin defends the choke while turning into your opponent and working the elbow-knee escape addresses the position itself rather than just the submission.
coach_note: Chin down buys you time to escape the position.

prompt: You're working the elbow-knee escape and manage to get your knee inside. Your opponent is heavy. What do you do with that inside knee?
options: Use it to push and create space, sliding toward half guard
Lock your ankles together under them
Push straight up with your knee toward the ceiling
correct_answer: Use it to push and create space, sliding toward half guard
explanation: Once your knee is inside, use it as a wedge to push their hip and create space to slide your leg through to half guard or full guard.
coach_note: Inside knee is a wedge — push and slide, don't just hold.

prompt: You're flat under mount. Your opponent has both your arms pinned to the mat. What's your priority?
options: Frame with your arms and turn on your side to start creating space
Keep your arms pinned and bridge straight up
Grab their belt and pull them forward
correct_answer: Frame with your arms and turn on your side to start creating space
explanation: With pinned arms you have no base for a proper upa. Getting on your side creates frames and makes the elbow-knee escape possible.
coach_note: Side position is almost always better than being flat.

prompt: You trap your opponent's arm and ankle and bridge hard. They start to roll. What do you do with your body?
options: Follow them over and land in their guard on top
Let go and try to stand up
Stop bridging and grab their collar
correct_answer: Follow them over and land in their guard on top
explanation: When the bridge works you must commit and follow through, rolling on top of them. Stopping mid-bridge lets them recover mount.
coach_note: Commit to the roll — halfway is worse than not going at all.

prompt: You're under mount and trying the upa. Your opponent keeps their elbow tight to their body so you can't trap their arm. What do you do?
options: Switch to the elbow-knee escape instead
Try to pry their arm away with both your hands
Bridge anyway without trapping
correct_answer: Switch to the elbow-knee escape instead
explanation: If you can't trap the arm, the upa won't work — don't waste energy. The elbow-knee escape doesn't require trapping the arm and is a natural alternative.
coach_note: Always have a backup — upa and elbow-knee work together.

prompt: You're under mount and your opponent is sitting up very tall. You feel their weight is light on you. What's the best option?
options: Shoot your knee inside immediately for the elbow-knee escape
Wait for them to come down before moving
Try to sit up and grab their neck
correct_answer: Shoot your knee inside immediately for the elbow-knee escape
explanation: When their weight is light, you have the most space to move. This is the ideal time to get your knee inside before they sit back down.
coach_note: Light pressure = your window to move. Take it.

prompt: You're escaping mount with the elbow-knee escape. You've reached half guard — your opponent has one leg trapped. What should you do?
options: Keep working to recover full guard or take the back
Stop and celebrate — you're safe in half guard
Bridge from half guard to try to sweep
correct_answer: Keep working to recover full guard or take the back
explanation: Half guard is progress but you're still on bottom in a bad position. Keep working to recover full guard or look for back takes — don't stall.
coach_note: Half guard is a step, not the destination.

prompt: You're under mount. Your opponent leans forward and puts their hands on the mat next to your head. What escape does this set up?
options: Upa — trap the arm that's on the mat and the same-side leg, then bridge
Elbow-knee escape — push your elbow to your knee
Grab their head and pull them into you
correct_answer: Upa — trap the arm that's on the mat and the same-side leg, then bridge
explanation: When your opponent posts their hand on the mat, they've given you an arm to trap. Secure the wrist and same-side ankle, then bridge explosively.
coach_note: They put a hand down — that's your invitation to upa.

prompt: You're under mount and you've trapped the arm and leg. You bridge but your opponent is much heavier. Should you still bridge?
options: Yes — bridge as explosively as possible and use your whole body, not just your hips
No — save energy and try elbow-knee instead
Yes — but bridge slowly to conserve energy
correct_answer: Yes — bridge as explosively as possible and use your whole body, not just your hips
explanation: The upa requires explosive power to overcome heavier opponents. A slow bridge won't work — use your legs to drive and your whole body to generate force.
coach_note: Explosive bridge beats strength difference. Slow = fail.

prompt: You're under mount trying the elbow-knee escape. Your opponent feels the space and drops their hip to block your knee. What do you do?
options: Try the upa on the other side — their weight shift may have created a trap opportunity
Keep forcing the knee inside against their resistance
Go completely flat and reset
correct_answer: Try the upa on the other side — their weight shift may have created a trap opportunity
explanation: When an opponent shifts to block one escape, they often open up the other. Their hip drop to stop the knee may leave an arm trappable for the upa.
coach_note: Two escapes, one opponent — they can only block one at a time.

prompt: You're under high mount. Your opponent is squeezing their knees into your armpits. You feel an armbar being set up. What's your immediate priority?
options: Clasp your hands together to prevent the arm from being isolated
Push their knee away with your free hand
Bridge immediately hoping to escape
correct_answer: Clasp your hands together to prevent the arm from being isolated
explanation: Clasping your hands keeps your arms connected and makes it much harder for your opponent to isolate one arm for the armbar. This buys you time to escape.
coach_note: Hands together = armbar defense first, then escape.

prompt: You're under mount. Your opponent starts walking their knees up toward your armpits. What does this signal and how should you respond?
options: They're going to high mount — act now with upa or elbow-knee before it gets worse
They're tired — wait for them to come back down
They're setting up a guard pass — don't worry
correct_answer: They're going to high mount — act now with upa or elbow-knee before it gets worse
explanation: High mount is worse than regular mount — escape becomes much harder once they get there. Act while they're transitioning and their balance is momentarily compromised.
coach_note: Stop the upgrade before it happens.

prompt: You successfully do the upa and roll your opponent. You end up on top but inside their guard. What's your first concern?
options: Posture up and work to pass their guard
Immediately drop back into mount to reverse the positions
Go for a quick choke from inside guard
correct_answer: Posture up and work to pass their guard
explanation: Being inside someone's guard is not a winning position but it's far better than being mounted. Posture up to stay safe and begin working your guard pass.
coach_note: You escaped — now don't give them your neck.

prompt: You're under mount and you've been stuck here for 30 seconds. You're getting tired. Should you try harder escapes or do something else?
options: Conserve energy by framing and staying on your side while looking for the right moment
Explode with everything you have repeatedly until you escape
Go flat and give up your arms to rest
correct_answer: Conserve energy by framing and staying on your side while looking for the right moment
explanation: Framing from your side prevents submissions while conserving energy. Explosive escapes need a moment — don't waste all your gas randomly thrashing.
coach_note: BJJ is a marathon. Frame up, breathe, pick your moment.

prompt: You're under mount on your side. You have a frame with your elbow and knee. Your opponent posts their hand on the mat right next to your hip. What should you do?
options: Trap their posted wrist and bridge immediately
Push their posted hand away
Ignore it and keep working the elbow-knee
correct_answer: Trap their posted wrist and bridge immediately
explanation: A posted hand on the mat is an arm to trap for the upa. Grab the wrist immediately — opportunities like this are fleeting.
coach_note: Any time they post a hand, think upa.

prompt: You're doing the upa escape. You've trapped the arm and leg. You bridge and roll them but you don't follow through — you stop halfway. What happens?
options: They recover back to mount on top of you
You end up in a scramble on equal footing
You end up in side control on top
correct_answer: They recover back to mount on top of you
explanation: A half-committed bridge gives your opponent time to post out and recover their position. You must follow all the way through and land on top.
coach_note: Go all in — hesitation means you stay on bottom.

prompt: You're under mount. Your opponent sits up and you see space between their body and yours. What do you do?
options: Immediately turn on your side and shoot your knee inside for the elbow-knee escape
Sit up with them
Push their hips away with both hands
correct_answer: Immediately turn on your side and shoot your knee inside for the elbow-knee escape
explanation: Space under mount is precious and disappears quickly. The moment you see space, turn and shoot your knee inside — don't hesitate.
coach_note: Space appears and disappears fast. React immediately.

prompt: You're under mount and your opponent grabs one of your wrists and pins it to the mat. What should you do?
options: Use your free arm to trap their pinning arm and set up the upa with your legs
Pull your pinned wrist free with full strength
Go limp so they think you're not a threat
correct_answer: Use your free arm to trap their pinning arm and set up the upa with your legs
explanation: When they grab your wrist, they've committed their arm close to your body — trap it with your free hand and set up the upa. Don't waste energy fighting the grip directly.
coach_note: Their grip on your wrist is the arm you trap.

prompt: You're under mount. Your opponent leans hard to one side trying to submit you. What does this create?
options: Space on the other side — shoot your knee inside on the open side for the elbow-knee escape
A chance to roll over — roll toward their lean
Nothing useful — wait until they sit back up
correct_answer: Space on the other side — shoot your knee inside on the open side for the elbow-knee escape
explanation: When your opponent leans hard one way, the opposite side opens up. This is your best window to get your knee inside on that open side.
coach_note: Their lean is your opening — look opposite.

prompt: You're under mount. Your opponent has S-mount — their knee is across your belly and they're turning toward your arm. What is happening?
options: They're setting up an armbar — clasp your hands and bridge immediately
They're moving to side control — wait and let them
They're trying a knee-on-belly — push their knee away
correct_answer: They're setting up an armbar — clasp your hands and bridge immediately
explanation: S-mount is a classic setup for the armbar. Clasp your hands together to prevent arm isolation and bridge urgently to break their balance.
coach_note: S-mount means armbar is coming. Clasp and bridge NOW.

prompt: You're under mount. You want to upa to your right. Which leg do you bridge with first?
options: Your right leg — plant your right foot and drive your hips up and to the left to roll them to your right
Your left leg — push from the opposite side
Both legs at the same time — push straight up
correct_answer: Your right leg — plant your right foot and drive your hips up and to the left to roll them to your right
explanation: To roll your opponent to your right, you drive off your right foot and bridge up-and-over to the left, taking them with you. The bridge goes toward the trapped side.
coach_note: Plant the foot on the side you want to roll toward.

prompt: You're under mount and your opponent wraps both arms around your head in a Gable grip. What is the risk and what do you do?
options: They're trying to flatten and control you — frame on their hip and work the elbow-knee escape
They want to roll you — go with the roll
They're going for a headlock choke — tap immediately
correct_answer: They're trying to flatten and control you — frame on their hip and work the elbow-knee escape
explanation: Wrapping your head is a control technique to keep you flat and prevent escapes. Don't panic — frame on their hip, create space, and work the elbow-knee escape.
coach_note: Frames on the hip break their control grip over time.

prompt: You're under mount and your elbow-knee escape gets you to half guard. Your opponent immediately flattens you out in half guard. What should you do?
options: Turn on your side, get an underhook, and keep fighting toward full guard
Bridge from half guard right away
Release their leg and give them side control
correct_answer: Turn on your side, get an underhook, and keep fighting toward full guard
explanation: Half guard on your back is dangerous — turn to your side, fight for the underhook, and keep working. An underhook is your key to recovering full guard or taking the back.
coach_note: Underhook in half guard is everything.

prompt: You're flat under mount and your opponent is chest-to-chest, very low, squeezing tight. Which escape is hardest to do in this position?
options: Elbow-knee escape — there's no space to get the knee inside
Upa — their low weight actually makes it harder to bridge
Both are equally easy
correct_answer: Elbow-knee escape — there's no space to get the knee inside
explanation: When an opponent goes very low and chest-to-chest, they eliminate the space needed to get your knee inside. The upa is actually more viable here because their weight is forward.
coach_note: Low tight mount = upa. No space = no elbow-knee.

prompt: You're under mount. Your opponent is controlling one of your arms across your chest. What is the danger?
options: They may be setting up an armbar or cross choke — control that arm and turn into them
Nothing — crossing an arm is harmless
They're trying to take your back — flatten yourself
correct_answer: They may be setting up an armbar or cross choke — control that arm and turn into them
explanation: A cross-armed position exposes you to both armbars and cross collar chokes. Acknowledge the threat, secure your arm, and work your escape with urgency.
coach_note: Arms crossing your center line = danger. Fix it fast.

prompt: You're under mount. You try the upa and your opponent doesn't roll but they do move forward slightly. What do you do?
options: Shoot your knee inside on the side they moved away from and go for elbow-knee escape
Try the upa again immediately in the same direction
Grab their collar and try to choke them
correct_answer: Shoot your knee inside on the side they moved away from and go for elbow-knee escape
explanation: A failed upa often makes your opponent shift forward, creating space behind them. That's your window to shoot your knee inside before they settle.
coach_note: Combine escapes — upa first, elbow-knee off the reaction.

prompt: You're under mount. Your opponent has mount but their feet are hooked under your legs (grapevines). How does this affect the upa?
options: It makes the upa harder — their hooks give them more stability, so focus on the elbow-knee escape
It makes the upa easier — you can use their hooks against them
It has no effect on your escapes
correct_answer: It makes the upa harder — their hooks give them more stability, so focus on the elbow-knee escape
explanation: Grapevine hooks flatten you and add stability to the mount, making the bridge harder to execute. The elbow-knee escape becomes the better option when grapevines are in.
coach_note: Grapevines = switch to elbow-knee.

prompt: You're under mount. Your opponent has a tight mount. You're on your side and your elbow is near your knee. What's your next move?
options: Use the frame to push their knee back and slide your bottom leg to recover guard
Lock your elbow and knee together and just hold the frame
Bridge from your side
correct_answer: Use the frame to push their knee back and slide your bottom leg to recover guard
explanation: The elbow-knee frame isn't just for defense — use it actively to push their knee back and create the space needed to slide your bottom leg through to half or full guard.
coach_note: The frame is active — push and slide, don't just hold.

prompt: You're under mount and about to upa. Your opponent senses it and starts to post their free hand. What should you do?
options: Bridge explosively right now before their hand reaches the mat
Wait until their hand is posted and try anyway
Switch immediately to elbow-knee
correct_answer: Bridge explosively right now before their hand reaches the mat
explanation: Once their hand posts on the mat you lose the mechanical advantage of the upa. Bridge immediately and explosively before they establish the post.
coach_note: Timing matters — bridge before the post, not after.

prompt: You just escaped mount using the upa and you're on top in guard. Your opponent pulls you down by the head. What do you do?
options: Posture up immediately — straighten your back and pull your head free
Stay low and try to submit from inside guard
Jump back to avoid guard entirely
correct_answer: Posture up immediately — straighten your back and pull your head free
explanation: Being pulled down inside guard exposes you to chokes and armbars. Posture up right away to stay safe and give yourself room to work the guard pass.
coach_note: Posture is your first job inside guard.

prompt: You're under mount and you can't escape. Your opponent starts to isolate your arm for a bent armlock (Americana). What should you do?
options: Tap before the lock is applied if you can't defend — don't get injured fighting the submission
Pull your arm free with all your strength
Bridge hard hoping to roll them
correct_answer: Tap before the lock is applied if you can't defend — don't get injured fighting the submission
explanation: At white belt your safety is the priority. If you can't prevent the lock and it's going on, tap early — fighting a fully applied Americana can injure your shoulder.
coach_note: Tap early, tap often. Live to train tomorrow.

prompt: You're under mount. Your opponent has mount and is trying to choke you with one hand pushing your chin. What do you do?
options: Tuck your chin, turn your head, and immediately start your escape
Grab their hand with both of yours and fight the choke
Tap — a chin choke is dangerous
correct_answer: Tuck your chin, turn your head, and immediately start your escape
explanation: Chin pushes are nuisances, not real chokes. Tuck your chin to neutralize it and focus your energy on escaping the position — that's the real solution.
coach_note: Chin down beats chin pushes. Escape the position.

prompt: You're under mount and you successfully get your knee inside and recover half guard. Your opponent immediately tries to pass your half guard. What's your first move?
options: Turn to your side and fight for the underhook — don't let them flatten you
Release their leg and pull guard
Bridge from half guard to try to sweep them
correct_answer: Turn to your side and fight for the underhook — don't let them flatten you
explanation: Being flattened in half guard is almost as bad as mount. Turn to your side immediately and battle for the underhook — it's your key to defending the pass and recovering guard.
coach_note: Side plus underhook in half guard = you're still in the fight.

prompt: You're under mount. Your opponent is very calm and not doing anything. You feel the weight getting heavier. What should you do?
options: Make something happen — shrimp to your side and start working the elbow-knee escape
Wait for them to attack before you respond
Go flat to rest and conserve energy
correct_answer: Make something happen — shrimp to your side and start working the elbow-knee escape
explanation: Being passive under mount means they control the pace and you tire out. Shrimping to your side is low energy but gets you into position to escape — always be working.
coach_note: Never just lie there. Always be moving, always be framing.

prompt: You have your opponent's back with hooks in and seatbelt grip. Your top arm is across their chest, bottom arm under their armpit. Where should you place your hooks to keep control?
options: Inside their thighs, toes pointed outward
Behind their knees, feet crossed
On top of their hips, ankles crossed
correct_answer: Inside their thighs, toes pointed outward
explanation: Hooks go inside the thighs to control the hips and prevent your opponent from sitting out or rolling. Crossing your feet gives them a chance to ankle lock you.
coach_note: Never cross your feet — it's a gift to your opponent.

prompt: You're in back control with seatbelt grip. Your opponent grabs your top (choking) arm with both hands and starts pulling it down. What do you do?
options: Fight to keep the arm in place and force the choke anyway
Slide your free bottom arm up to their neck and start the rear naked choke from that side
Release the seatbelt and switch to a headlock
correct_answer: Slide your free bottom arm up to their neck and start the rear naked choke from that side
explanation: When they grab your top arm, your bottom arm becomes free to sneak up under the chin. Use their distraction against them by attacking from the other side.
coach_note: Their hands busy grabbing one arm means the other arm is open — take advantage.

prompt: You're in back control. You slide your arm under your opponent's chin to set up the rear naked choke, but your hand can't clasp — their chin is blocking. What do you do?
options: Force the arm deeper and squeeze anyway
Use your other hand to push their head forward so your choking arm can slide under the chin
Give up on the choke and go for a body lock instead
correct_answer: Use your other hand to push their head forward so your choking arm can slide under the chin
explanation: Pushing the head forward with your free hand creates the space needed for your choking arm to clear the chin and reach throat level for the rear naked choke.
coach_note: The chin is the main barrier — move the head, create the lane.

prompt: You're attacking the rear naked choke from back control. You have one arm under the chin. What is the correct way to finish the choke?
options: Grab your own wrist and squeeze both arms together
Grab your choking arm's bicep with your free hand and place that hand behind their head, then squeeze
Interlock your fingers behind their neck and pull
correct_answer: Grab your choking arm's bicep with your free hand and place that hand behind their head, then squeeze
explanation: The rear naked choke is finished by grabbing your own bicep, placing your free hand behind their head, and squeezing — this creates a tight triangle of pressure on both carotid arteries.
coach_note: Head behind the hand, not on top — that's what locks the choke in.

prompt: You're in back control and your opponent starts turning INTO your top arm, trying to face you. What is your first priority?
options: Let them turn and take guard
Rotate with them to stay on their back and keep your hooks in
Release your hooks and flatten them to the mat
correct_answer: Rotate with them to stay on their back and keep your hooks in
explanation: You must move with your opponent to stay on their back — back control is a dominant position and losing it means losing your attack. Rotate your body to mirror their movement.
coach_note: Think of yourself as a backpack — move everywhere they move.

prompt: You're in back control and your opponent manages to turn to face you, ending up in your closed guard. What went wrong?
options: Your choke was too loose
You let them turn without rotating your body to follow them
Your hooks were too high on their body
correct_answer: You let them turn without rotating your body to follow them
explanation: Allowing your opponent to turn without matching their rotation is the main reason back control is lost. You must stay chest-to-back by continuously adjusting your angle.
coach_note: Prevention is better than recovery — don't let them turn in the first place.

prompt: You have back control with seatbelt grip. Your opponent is very defensive — arms tight, chin down. What should you do to open up the choke?
options: Wait patiently for them to move on their own
Use your top arm to pry their elbow away from their body, then shoot the choking arm under the chin
Switch to double underhooks and try to suplex them
correct_answer: Use your top arm to pry their elbow away from their body, then shoot the choking arm under the chin
explanation: When your opponent is defending tightly, prying their elbow off their body creates the opening needed to sneak your arm to their throat. Force the opening rather than waiting.
coach_note: Tight defense just means you need to create the gap — pry, then shoot.

prompt: You're in back control. Your opponent bucks their hips explosively upward and your top hook pops out. What should you do immediately?
options: Re-insert your hook by hooking their inner thigh again before they can flatten you out
Let both hooks go and transition to side control
Squeeze your knees together to compensate
correct_answer: Re-insert your hook by hooking their inner thigh again before they can flatten you out
explanation: One lost hook must be recovered immediately — without both hooks, your opponent can flatten out and escape. Act fast before they exploit the opening.
coach_note: Speed matters here — one missing hook is an escape in progress.

prompt: You're in back control but your opponent is very tall and you're struggling to keep your hooks in. A teammate suggests a body triangle. Where do you lock it?
options: Your legs wrap around their waist, one shin in front, the other leg's foot tucked behind that knee
Both feet locked together behind their back
One knee on the mat, one foot hooked inside their thigh
correct_answer: Your legs wrap around their waist, one shin in front, the other leg's foot tucked behind that knee
explanation: The body triangle wraps one shin across the front of their midsection and locks it by tucking your other foot behind that knee, creating powerful hip control that is hard to escape.
coach_note: Body triangle is great against bigger opponents — it's harder to remove than hooks.

prompt: You're in back control trying to choke. Your opponent tucks their chin hard to their chest and you cannot get your arm under it. What do you do?
options: Keep ramming your arm at their chin with more force
Attack the neck from a different angle by working your forearm across their throat from the side while pulling their head back
Give up the choke and try to take mount instead
correct_answer: Attack the neck from a different angle by working your forearm across their throat from the side while pulling their head back
explanation: When the chin is locked down, a direct attack won't work. Use your forearm to apply pressure from the side and use your other hand or elbow to lift their head, breaking the chin-down defense.
coach_note: A stubborn chin means change the angle — don't fight force with force.

prompt: You have back control with seatbelt grip. Should your top arm or your bottom arm be the one that goes under the chin for the rear naked choke?
options: Top arm (the one across their chest)
Bottom arm (the one under their armpit)
Either arm — it doesn't matter
correct_answer: Top arm (the one across their chest)
explanation: The top arm of the seatbelt is already positioned near the neck and slides naturally under the chin to become the choking arm. The bottom arm then supports by grabbing the bicep.
coach_note: Top arm chokes, bottom arm locks — remember that setup every time.

prompt: You're in back control and your opponent suddenly rolls to the side, stacking you onto your back with them on top of you. You still have hooks and seatbelt. What do you do?
options: Let go completely and recover guard
Keep your hooks and seatbelt tight — you are still in back control even with them on top of you
Try to push them off with your feet
correct_answer: Keep your hooks and seatbelt tight — you are still in back control even with them on top of you
explanation: Back control is maintained as long as you have hooks and seatbelt, regardless of who is on top. Stay tight and continue attacking the choke from this inverted position.
coach_note: Position is about where your chest is relative to their back — not who's on top.

prompt: You just locked in the rear naked choke — arm under the chin, hand on bicep, free hand behind their head. Your opponent is not tapping yet. What should you do?
options: Crank harder and push their head forward with your chest
Squeeze both elbows toward each other and extend your chest into their back to tighten the choke
Shake the choke rapidly to make it tighter
correct_answer: Squeeze both elbows toward each other and extend your chest into their back to tighten the choke
explanation: Driving the elbows together compresses both sides of the neck while extending the chest into their back pushes their head forward into the choke — this maximizes carotid pressure.
coach_note: Squeeze in, push out — elbows together, chest forward. That's the finish.

prompt: Opponent just took your back and is setting up a rear naked choke. What's the very first thing you must do?
options: Tuck your chin and grab their choking arm with both hands
Try to roll forward immediately
Reach back and grab their head
correct_answer: Tuck your chin and grab their choking arm with both hands
explanation: Protecting the neck is the absolute first priority — without securing their choking arm, any escape attempt leaves you vulnerable to being choked.
