coach_note: Chin down, both hands on the choking arm — neck first, escape second.

prompt: You're defending back control and opponent has a seatbelt grip. Which direction should you try to turn to escape?
options: Turn toward the arm that is over your shoulder (the choking arm side)
Turn away from both arms
Turn toward the arm that is under your armpit
correct_answer: Turn toward the arm that is over your shoulder (the choking arm side)
explanation: Turning toward the choking arm traps it and prevents the choke while allowing you to work toward guard — turning away exposes your neck.
coach_note: Always turn INTO the choke arm, not away from it.

prompt: You're on your back with opponent behind you. They slide their arm under your chin. What do you do right now?
options: Tuck your chin hard and pull their arm down with both hands
Bridge immediately to escape
Grab their legs to defend
correct_answer: Tuck your chin hard and pull their arm down with both hands
explanation: The moment an arm goes under your chin you must defend the neck — chin tuck stops the choke from being secured while both hands control their arm.
coach_note: Chin to chest the instant you feel their arm moving toward your neck.

prompt: Opponent has your back with both hooks in. You've protected your neck. What's your next goal?
options: Remove one of their hooks so you can start turning
Try to stand up with them on your back
Go completely flat on your stomach
correct_answer: Remove one of their hooks so you can start turning
explanation: Removing a hook takes away their control and opens the path to turning into them or sliding to half guard — standing gives them full control.
coach_note: Hooks give them power — eliminate one before you try to turn.

prompt: You're defending back control and opponent is trying to flatten you onto your back. You feel their weight pushing down. What should you do?
options: Fight to stay on your side and keep your hips angled
Go flat on your back to relieve the pressure
Roll onto your stomach
correct_answer: Fight to stay on your side and keep your hips angled
explanation: Staying on your side prevents them from fully locking in hooks and keeps your escape options open — going flat gives them dominant position.
coach_note: Stay on your side as long as possible — flat on your back is dangerous.

prompt: Opponent has your back. You've grabbed their choking arm and tucked your chin. Now what do you do with your hips?
options: Slide your hips away from them and toward the mat
Keep your hips still so they can't move
Pull your knees up to your chest
correct_answer: Slide your hips away from them and toward the mat
explanation: Sliding your hips out creates space to turn and removes their hip-to-hip connection — staying still lets them maintain control.
coach_note: Hip escape is the engine of back escapes — move those hips!

prompt: You're trying to escape back control and you've turned slightly toward your opponent. They immediately grab for a triangle choke. What does this mean you did wrong?
options: You turned the wrong direction — away from the choking arm instead of toward it
You moved your hips too fast
You forgot to remove the hooks first
correct_answer: You turned the wrong direction — away from the choking arm instead of toward it
explanation: Turning away from the choking arm exposes your neck and arms to submissions — you must always turn toward the choking arm side.
coach_note: Check your direction before you turn — wrong way means a choke or triangle.

prompt: Opponent has your back with a body triangle instead of hooks. You can feel them squeezing your ribs. What's your first concern?
options: Protect your neck first, then work on the body triangle
Try to pry the body triangle open right away
Go flat on your stomach to relieve pressure
correct_answer: Protect your neck first, then work on the body triangle
explanation: A body triangle is uncomfortable but won't finish you — a choke will, so the neck is always the first priority.
coach_note: Body triangle is control, not a submission — protect your neck first.

prompt: Opponent has a body triangle on your midsection. You've secured your neck. How do you start attacking the body triangle?
options: Work to move toward the side where their feet are locked to try to create space
Try to stand up with them on your back
Grab their feet and pull them apart
correct_answer: Work to move toward the side where their feet are locked to try to create space
explanation: Moving toward the locked side puts mechanical pressure on the triangle and can help you create space to escape — pulling their feet apart is ineffective.
coach_note: Move toward the lock, not away from it.

prompt: You're in back control bottom and manage to slide one of their hooks off your leg. What should you do next?
options: Use that freed leg to push their remaining hook off and turn to your side
Stand up immediately
Try to sweep them over your head
correct_answer: Use that freed leg to push their remaining hook off and turn to your side
explanation: Once one hook is off, use your freed leg to clear the second hook while turning — standing gives them back control on the feet.
coach_note: Two hooks gone means you can turn — do it quickly.

prompt: Opponent tightens their rear naked choke and you feel it going on. You still have a little space. What do you do?
options: Tuck your chin into the crook of their elbow and pull their arm down
Tap immediately
Try to bridge and roll
correct_answer: Tuck your chin into the crook of their elbow and pull their arm down
explanation: Tucking into the crook of their elbow blocks the choke from tightening on your throat — you still have time to defend if you act immediately.
coach_note: Chin in the crook kills the choke — do it before it's too late.

prompt: You've escaped back control and landed in half guard on your side. Opponent is on top. What should you do?
options: Stay on your side and begin working to full guard or underhook
Roll to your back and rest
Try to stand up
correct_answer: Stay on your side and begin working to full guard or underhook
explanation: Half guard on your side is a functional defensive position — staying on your side prevents them from flattening you and keeps your escape options open.
coach_note: Half guard on your side is safe — don't let them flatten you.

prompt: Opponent has your back and you're on your side. You try to turn toward their choking arm but they flatten you onto your back. Now what?
options: Bridge hard to one side to get back on your side
Stay flat and try to peel their arms
Try to roll to your stomach
correct_answer: Bridge hard to one side to get back on your side
explanation: Bridging helps you recover your side position which is essential for back escapes — staying flat makes it much harder to escape.
coach_note: Never stay flat — bridge to recover your side position.

prompt: You're in back control bottom and opponent moves one hook to the outside of your thigh. What does this change about your escape?
options: It gives you a chance to trap that leg and slide to half guard more easily
It means you should turn away from them now
It means you should stand up
correct_answer: It gives you a chance to trap that leg and slide to half guard more easily
explanation: When a hook comes to the outside of your thigh, you can trap their leg with yours and slide your hips out toward half guard.
coach_note: Misplaced hooks are your opportunity — feel for them and react.

prompt: Opponent is on your back and you feel them shifting their weight to set up a bow and arrow choke. What's your immediate reaction?
options: Grab your own collar and tuck your chin to deny the grip
Try to roll forward
Go completely limp to confuse them
correct_answer: Grab your own collar and tuck your chin to deny the grip
explanation: Grabbing your own collar prevents them from getting the bow and arrow grip — your collar is their handle, so protect it.
coach_note: Protect your collar with your own grip — deny the bow and arrow setup.

prompt: You're defending back control and your chin is tucked but opponent is trying to pry it up with their hand. What do you do?
options: Grab their wrist with your free hand to stop them from prying your chin up
Open your chin to relieve the pressure
Try to bite their hand
correct_answer: Grab their wrist with your free hand to stop them from prying your chin up
explanation: Controlling their wrist stops the chin pry — never give up the chin tuck because the choke goes on immediately.
coach_note: Two hands defending the neck — one on their arm, chin always tucked.

prompt: Opponent has your back and you're working to escape. They start squeezing their hooks very tight together. What does this usually mean?
options: They're trying to keep control — you need to shrimp your hips to break their connection
They're about to submit you with their legs
You should stop moving
correct_answer: They're trying to keep control — you need to shrimp your hips to break their connection
explanation: Tight hooks mean they're fighting to maintain back control — shrimping creates the space you need to remove hooks and escape.
coach_note: Shrimp into their hooks, don't fight them with muscle.

prompt: You've turned to face your opponent and are now chest-to-chest. Where are you?
options: You've completed the back escape and are now in their guard
You're still in back control
You're in side control bottom
correct_answer: You've completed the back escape and are now in their guard
explanation: Turning to face your opponent chest-to-chest means you've successfully escaped back control — you're now in their guard.
coach_note: Chest to chest means back escape complete — now pass or control.

prompt: Opponent has your back with a tight seatbelt. You try to escape but they keep pulling you back. What part of their control are you forgetting to address?
options: The seatbelt grip — you need to break or manage it while escaping
Their legs — you need to remove hooks first always
Their head position
correct_answer: The seatbelt grip — you need to break or manage it while escaping
explanation: The seatbelt grip controls your upper body and pulls you back — you must work to break or loosen it while escaping the hooks.
coach_note: Address the seatbelt and hooks together — both must be managed.

prompt: You're in back control bottom and starting to panic. What's the most important thing to remember?
options: Breathe, protect your neck, and move methodically
Burst out with full energy immediately
Go completely limp and wait
correct_answer: Breathe, protect your neck, and move methodically
explanation: Panic burns energy and leads to mistakes — controlled breathing and methodical movement will get you out much more reliably than frantic bursting.
coach_note: Calm down, chin down, work your escape step by step.

prompt: Opponent has your back and their top hook is very high on your hip. You feel you can slide your bottom hip out. What should you do?
options: Shrimp your hips out toward the mat and start sliding to half guard
Bridge upward first
Post your elbow and try to stand
correct_answer: Shrimp your hips out toward the mat and start sliding to half guard
explanation: A high hook creates space at the hip — shrimping into that space and sliding down is the path to half guard and eventually full escape.
coach_note: Space at the hip means shrimp — slide out to half guard.

prompt: Opponent locks in a rear naked choke and you feel blood flow restricted. You have a fraction of a second. What do you do?
options: Tap immediately — never let a choke go to completion
Try to escape one last time
Turn your head to the side
correct_answer: Tap immediately — never let a choke go to completion
explanation: If the choke is fully locked and you feel it working, tap — going unconscious is dangerous and there is no shame in tapping.
coach_note: Tap early and tap often — your safety comes first.

prompt: You're in back control bottom. Opponent is higher on your back than usual — almost at your neck. What do you do differently?
options: Slide your hips downward and away to get back to proper alignment
Try to turn toward their choking arm immediately
Go to your stomach
correct_answer: Slide your hips downward and away to get back to proper alignment
explanation: When they ride too high, their hooks lose effectiveness — slide your hips down to create misalignment and make their position uncomfortable.
coach_note: Make them uncomfortable by sliding out from under their weight.

prompt: You're defending back control and you successfully remove both hooks. Opponent still has the seatbelt. What do you do?
options: Turn toward the choking arm and come chest to chest
Stand up quickly
Try to roll forward
correct_answer: Turn toward the choking arm and come chest to chest
explanation: With hooks gone, you have the mobility to turn — rotating toward the choking arm gets you to chest-to-chest and completes the back escape.
coach_note: Hooks gone means it's time to turn — go toward the choking arm.

prompt: Opponent keeps switching from trying to choke you to trying to flatten your weight onto the mat. How should you deal with this?
options: Keep one hand protecting the neck while fighting to stay on your side with your body
Alternate between defending each threat one at a time
Stop moving and conserve energy
correct_answer: Keep one hand protecting the neck while fighting to stay on your side with your body
explanation: Neck protection is constant — you use your body mechanics to stay on your side, not your hands, so one hand can always guard the neck.
coach_note: Multi-task: neck hand stays, body does the side fighting.

prompt: You're on bottom of back control and you try shrimping your hips out. Opponent responds by pulling you back with the seatbelt. What do you do?
options: Use their pulling energy — time your shrimp with a bridge to create momentum
Stop shrimping and wait
Try to grab their leg with your hand
correct_answer: Use their pulling energy — time your shrimp with a bridge to create momentum
explanation: Working with their pull by bridging at the right moment creates momentum they can't easily stop — fighting against a pull directly wastes energy.
coach_note: Use their energy against them — bridge when they pull.

prompt: Opponent has your back and you feel them trying to slide their knee under your thigh to improve their hook. What should you do immediately?
options: Drive your knee down to prevent them from getting the hook in deeper
Let them set the hook and deal with it later
Try to stand up
correct_answer: Drive your knee down to prevent them from getting the hook in deeper
explanation: A deep hook gives them much more control — stopping the hook from going deeper while you still have the chance is easier than removing it later.
coach_note: Fight the hook going in early — it's much harder to remove a deep hook.

prompt: You've escaped to half guard from back control. Your opponent quickly flattens you onto your back. What's the danger?
options: Flat on your back in half guard means they can pass to side control easily
There is no danger — half guard bottom is safe flat
They can apply a rear naked choke from there
correct_answer: Flat on your back in half guard means they can pass to side control easily
explanation: Being flat on your back in half guard makes it easy for your opponent to free their leg and pass to side control — stay on your side.
coach_note: Half guard works from your side — flat on your back is dangerous.

prompt: Opponent is on your back and you're using the elbow-knee escape. You've freed your hips but their arm is still around your neck loosely. What do you do?
options: Keep the choking arm controlled until you're fully turned chest to chest
Release their arm and turn as fast as possible
Celebrate — the escape is done
correct_answer: Keep the choking arm controlled until you're fully turned chest to chest
explanation: A loose choking arm can be tightened in a split second — keep controlling it all the way until you're fully facing them.
coach_note: Don't release their arm until you're fully chest to chest.

prompt: You're in back control and opponent slides their arm across your shoulder blades behind your head. What are they setting up?
options: A choke using the collar or a variation — protect your neck immediately
A shoulder lock — protect your shoulder
Nothing dangerous — keep escaping normally
correct_answer: A choke using the collar or a variation — protect your neck immediately
explanation: An arm across the shoulder blades behind your head is often the beginning of a collar choke setup — tuck chin and control it immediately.
coach_note: Any arm near your head area means choke danger — react fast.

prompt: You're in back control bottom and feel very tired. Opponent is patiently waiting. What's your best approach?
options: Keep moving small — tiny hip escapes and constant pressure on their grip
Wait for your energy to come back before moving
Give up position and focus on surviving
correct_answer: Keep moving small — tiny hip escapes and constant pressure on their grip
explanation: Small constant movement creates micro-opportunities and prevents them from settling — stopping completely gives them time to improve position.
coach_note: Small consistent movement beats big bursts when you're tired.

prompt: Opponent has your back and they're much bigger and stronger than you. They're easily resisting your escape attempts. What should you focus on?
options: Use technique — hip escapes and proper mechanics rather than strength
Try harder and use more strength
Go flat and survive
correct_answer: Use technique — hip escapes and proper mechanics rather than strength
explanation: Technique and leverage work regardless of size difference — fighting strength with strength against a bigger opponent is a losing battle.
coach_note: Technique beats strength — trust the mechanics.

prompt: You're in back control bottom. Opponent has a seatbelt with their right arm over and left arm under. Which side should you turn toward?
options: Turn toward your right — toward the arm that is over your shoulder
Turn toward your left — toward the arm under your armpit
Either side is fine
correct_answer: Turn toward your right — toward the arm that is over your shoulder
explanation: The arm over your shoulder is the choking arm — you must turn toward it to trap it and prevent the choke, not away from it.
coach_note: Turn toward the over-arm — that's the choking arm and your escape direction.

prompt: Opponent takes your back and immediately flattens you. You're now flat on your back with them on top. What's wrong with this position for you?
options: It's very dangerous — their hooks are easier to use and choke is easier to set up
It's fine — you can escape from flat on your back easily
It means they've already submitted you
correct_answer: It's very dangerous — their hooks are easier to use and choke is easier to set up
explanation: Being flat on your back gives them maximum control — hooks work better and they can establish a choke much more easily from this position.
coach_note: Never stay flat — getting to your side is job one.

prompt: You're trying to escape back control and you push one of their legs down to remove a hook. They resist strongly. What should you do?
options: Don't fight it — look for the other hook or wait for a better moment
Keep pushing harder to force the hook off
Let go and try to stand up instead
correct_answer: Don't fight it — look for the other hook or wait for a better moment
explanation: If they're committed to defending one hook, the other may be weaker or you can wait for their focus to shift — fighting a strong resistance directly wastes energy.
coach_note: Don't fight what's defended — find the weak point.

prompt: Opponent has your back and starts walking their hooks up toward your knees to change their position. What should you do?
options: Use this moment of movement to shrimp your hips and start your escape
Wait until they settle before trying to escape
Grab their feet to stop them
correct_answer: Use this moment of movement to shrimp your hips and start your escape
explanation: When they're moving their hooks they have less control momentarily — this is your window to shrimp and create escape momentum.
coach_note: Their movement is your opportunity — react to transitions.

prompt: You've escaped back control and ended up in their closed guard. What should your immediate mindset be?
options: Stay calm — closed guard bottom is much safer than back control bottom
Try to escape guard immediately and aggressively
Give them your back again to reset
correct_answer: Stay calm — closed guard bottom is much safer than back control bottom
explanation: Ending up in closed guard after escaping back control is a big improvement — it's a much safer position and you've done your job.
coach_note: Closed guard is a win compared to back control — take the improvement.

prompt: Opponent has your back and you've tucked your chin but they're extremely strong and starting to get the choke despite your defense. What now?
options: Keep fighting the choke with your hands while working a hip escape simultaneously
Give up the chin tuck and bridge instead
Stop defending and tap right away
correct_answer: Keep fighting the choke with your hands while working a hip escape simultaneously
explanation: You must defend the choke AND work the escape at the same time — giving up the chin or stopping movement makes it easier for them to finish.
coach_note: Fight the choke and escape at the same time — don't do only one.

prompt: You're in back control bottom and opponent suddenly releases their hooks to try a different attack. What do you do?
options: Immediately turn toward the choking arm and work to get chest to chest
Wait to see what they try next
Put your hands on the mat and try to stand
correct_answer: Immediately turn toward the choking arm and work to get chest to chest
explanation: Released hooks are an immediate escape opportunity — you must react immediately to turn and face them before they re-establish control.
coach_note: React instantly to open windows — hesitation means they recover.

prompt: You're defending back control and you feel the choke going tighter no matter what you do. You're seconds from going out. What is the right decision?
options: Tap clearly and loudly right now
Try one more escape
Go limp and hope they let go
correct_answer: Tap clearly and loudly right now
explanation: When the choke is on and working, tap immediately — there is nothing to gain from going unconscious and your safety is always the priority.
coach_note: Tapping is smart — going out in training serves no purpose.

prompt: You're passing open guard and your opponent grabs both your sleeves with a spider guard grip. What's your first priority?
options: Strip their grips before attempting any pass
Step back and wait for them to release
Try to pass immediately while they have the grips
correct_answer: Strip their grips before attempting any pass
explanation: Spider guard controls your arms and posture — you cannot pass effectively until those grips are broken. Attempting to pass with active spider grips lets them extend and off-balance you.
coach_note: Grips first, pass second — always.

prompt: Your opponent has spider guard and is pushing one foot into your bicep, extending your arm. What do you do?
options: Step your elbow down and in to collapse the frame
Pull your arm straight back against their leg push
Reach over and grab their collar instead
correct_answer: Step your elbow down and in to collapse the frame
explanation: Dropping your elbow inward breaks the leverage of the bicep push and collapses their frame. Pulling straight back actually gives them more extension to work with.
coach_note: Don't fight their push — collapse it by changing the angle.

prompt: You grab both of your opponent's ankles for a toreando pass. Which direction do you move their legs?
options: Push both legs to one side while you move to the other side
Push both legs straight down to the mat
Pull both legs toward you while staying in front
correct_answer: Push both legs to one side while you move to the other side
explanation: The toreando works by redirecting the legs one way so you can move your body the opposite direction, bypassing the guard. Staying in front doesn't create the angle you need.
coach_note: Legs go that way, you go the other way.

prompt: You're mid-toreando pass and your opponent turns toward you to re-guard. What do you do?
options: Follow their turn and continue around to their back
Stop and reset back to standing
Push their legs back down and try again from the front
correct_answer: Follow their turn and continue around to their back
explanation: When your opponent turns to face you during the toreando, their back is now available — following their turn and going behind is often easier than starting over.
coach_note: Their turn creates a back-take opportunity — don't waste it.

prompt: Your opponent has collar-sleeve guard and pulls your sleeve while pushing your collar. You're getting broken down to one side. What should you do?
options: Posture up and strip the sleeve grip first
Lean into their pull to pass that direction
Sit down to avoid being swept
correct_answer: Posture up and strip the sleeve grip first
explanation: Collar-sleeve grip breaks your posture by pulling your sleeve and pushing your collar — posturing up and removing the sleeve grip kills their leverage. Leaning in feeds the sweep.
coach_note: Posture is your best defense against collar-sleeve control.

prompt: You're trying to pass and your opponent has a lasso guard on your right arm. What's your immediate concern?
options: Your right arm is trapped and they can sweep or triangle you
You should push their hips down to flatten them
You should grab their collar with your free hand immediately
correct_answer: Your right arm is trapped and they can sweep or triangle you
explanation: Lasso guard locks your arm inside their leg, creating serious sweep and submission threats. Recognizing this danger is the first step — you must work to free that arm before advancing.
coach_note: Lasso on your arm means danger — don't ignore it.

prompt: Your opponent has lasso guard on your arm. How do you start to free it?
options: Rotate your elbow upward and pull your arm out of the lasso
Yank your arm straight back forcefully
Pass to the other side with the trapped arm still in
correct_answer: Rotate your elbow upward and pull your arm out of the lasso
explanation: The lasso depends on your arm being threaded through their leg — rotating the elbow upward reverses that thread and lets you extract the arm cleanly.
coach_note: Elbow up to undo the lasso — it's like unscrewing a bottle cap.

prompt: You're attempting the smash pass (leg weave) and have your opponent's legs stacked. They start bridging into you. What do you do?
options: Lower your hips and drive your chest into them to maintain the smash
Back off and let them recover guard
Stand up to avoid being rolled
correct_answer: Lower your hips and drive your chest into them to maintain the smash
explanation: When your opponent bridges during a smash pass, you counter by lowering your center of gravity and driving your weight forward — this smothers the bridge and keeps the stack.
coach_note: Stay low and heavy — don't let them create space with the bridge.

prompt: You're setting up the leg weave pass and you have their outside leg controlled. Where do you put your head?
options: Press your head into their thigh on the same side as the weave
Keep your head up and away from their body
Put your head on their chest
correct_answer: Press your head into their thigh on the same side as the weave
explanation: Head pressure into the thigh during the leg weave smash keeps their hips flat and prevents them from shrimping or recovering guard. Keeping your head up loses that important control point.
coach_note: Head pressure is what makes the smash pass actually smash.

prompt: Your opponent extends both legs into your hips to create distance when you try to pass. What do you do?
options: Step to the side to make the extension less effective
Try to bulldoze straight through their leg extension
Grab their ankles and sit down between their legs
correct_answer: Step to the side to make the extension less effective
explanation: Leg extensions push you straight back — moving laterally removes your body from the line of force and lets you pass around rather than through.
coach_note: Don't fight straight into a leg push — get off the line.

prompt: You're passing open guard from standing and your opponent keeps scooting toward you and replacing their guard. What adjustment do you make?
options: Move faster and commit to your pass with more explosive footwork
Wait for them to stop moving
Get down to your knees to slow the exchange
correct_answer: Move faster and commit to your pass with more explosive footwork
explanation: A mobile guard player recovers because you give them time — committing to the pass with explosive movement beats their recovery before it happens.
coach_note: Hesitation is your enemy when passing open guard.

prompt: You're on top and your opponent grabs your collar with one hand and your sleeve with the other. You feel them pulling you forward and to the side. What do you do?
options: Posture up and break the collar grip before it gets worse
Shoot your hips forward and try to knee-slide past
Grab their belt and pull up to counter
correct_answer: Posture up and break the collar grip before it gets worse
explanation: Collar-sleeve control breaks your posture to set up sweeps — posturing up immediately stops the sweep attempt and lets you work to strip grips.
coach_note: Posture breaks = sweep incoming. Stand tall immediately.

prompt: You grab both ankles for a toreando and your opponent sits up toward you as you try to pass. What do you do?
options: Push their legs into them to knock them back and continue the pass
Let go of the ankles and sprawl back
Grab their head instead
correct_answer: Push their legs into them to knock them back and continue the pass
explanation: When a guard player sits up during the toreando, you can use their own movement against them by shoving their legs back — this disrupts the sit-up and lets you continue passing.
coach_note: Use their sit-up energy against them by pushing legs back into them.

prompt: You've pinned one of your opponent's legs to the mat and are moving to pass. They bring their other knee in between you to block your pass. What do you do?
options: Use your forearm to push that knee back out of the way
Back off and try a different pass entirely
Try to grab that knee and hold it
correct_answer: Use your forearm to push that knee back out of the way
explanation: The knee shield is a recovery tool — clearing it with your forearm keeps you on track to complete the pass rather than getting stalled out.
coach_note: The knee coming in means they're trying to recover — don't let it.

prompt: Your opponent has open guard and keeps moving their hips away every time you try to engage. What is your best approach?
options: Cut the distance and grab their pants or ankles before they can move away
Chase their hips with yours and match their movements
Stand back and bait them into attacking
correct_answer: Cut the distance and grab their pants or ankles before they can move away
explanation: A mobile guard player uses distance to reset — closing the distance quickly and establishing grips takes that option away from them.
coach_note: Grips stop the movement — get your hands on them first.

prompt: You're passing and your opponent gets a deep de la riva hook on your lead leg. What's your immediate response?
options: Step your hooked leg back and to the outside to clear the hook
Keep moving forward and try to drag them with you
Drop to your knee on the hooked side
correct_answer: Step your hooked leg back and to the outside to clear the hook
explanation: The de la riva hook controls your lead leg and puts you in danger of being swept — stepping back and out removes the hook and lets you re-establish your base.
coach_note: Clear the hook before doing anything else — it controls your movement.

prompt: You have good posture on top and your opponent is using butterfly guard with hooks. They suddenly sit up and underhook your arm. What do you do?
options: Sprawl your hips back and flatten them before they can complete the sweep
Try to grab their collar to hold them down
Post your hand on the mat and wait to see what they do
correct_answer: Sprawl your hips back and flatten them before they can complete the sweep
explanation: A butterfly underhook with a sit-up is the setup for a butterfly sweep — sprawling your hips back kills the lift they need to sweep you.
coach_note: Hips back stops the butterfly lift every time.

prompt: You're standing over your opponent's open guard and they are placing their feet on your hips. What grip should you establish before they can push you away?
options: Grab their pants at the knees or ankles to control their legs
Grab their collar to keep them close
Don't grab anything and step back to avoid the feet
correct_answer: Grab their pants at the knees or ankles to control their legs
explanation: Feet on hips means they're about to extend and push you away — grabbing their legs first neutralizes the push and gives you control of their guard.
coach_note: Control the legs before they can use them to push you away.

prompt: You're in a toreando position and have pushed your opponent's legs to their right. You move to their left side to pass. As you get close, they start to turn into you. How fast do you need to establish side control?
options: Immediately — get your chest down and hip in before they can face you
Take your time, the pass is already done
Wait until they stop moving, then settle in
correct_answer: Immediately — get your chest down and hip in before they can face you
explanation: Once the legs are cleared, every second you wait gives your opponent time to recover guard — pinning your hip into them and getting your chest down immediately secures the pass.
coach_note: Finish the pass aggressively — don't admire your work.

prompt: You're trying to pass open guard and your opponent plants one foot on your hip and the other on your bicep. They push hard and you stumble back. What do you focus on first when you recover?
options: Regain your grip on their legs before re-engaging
Rush back in immediately and try the same pass
Look for a submission from the outside
correct_answer: Regain your grip on their legs before re-engaging
explanation: Rushing back in without grips puts you back in the same problem — establishing grip control on their legs first means you dictate the next engagement.
coach_note: Grips before re-engaging — every single time.

prompt: You're passing with a knee slide and your opponent frames their shin across your hips to block. What do you do?
options: Drive your knee through and flatten their hip to clear the frame
Stop the knee slide and try to go around the other direction
Grab their shin and pull it away before continuing
correct_answer: Drive your knee through and flatten their hip to clear the frame
explanation: The shin frame on the hip is meant to stall your knee slide — driving through it by flattening their hip removes the frame and allows the pass to continue.
coach_note: Flatten the hip and the shin frame loses its power.

prompt: Your opponent has collar-sleeve guard and uses it to keep your arm extended and your posture broken. Every time you try to pull your arm back, they just re-extend it. What should you try?
options: Step forward aggressively to close the distance and collapse the extension
Keep pulling your arm back harder
Switch to grabbing their leg with your free hand
correct_answer: Step forward aggressively to close the distance and collapse the extension
explanation: The collar-sleeve extension relies on distance — stepping forward removes that space and the extension collapses, letting you take back control of your posture.
coach_note: The cure for extension is closing distance, not pulling away.

prompt: You're attempting a toreando pass and just as you push the legs to the side, your opponent grabs your ankle with their hand. What do you do?
options: Keep moving around their guard — the ankle grab won't stop you if you move fast
Stop immediately and free the ankle before doing anything
Jump over their legs to the other side
correct_answer: Keep moving around their guard — the ankle grab won't stop you if you move fast
explanation: An ankle grab from a guard player on their back doesn't have much structural power — committing to the toreando movement quickly makes it irrelevant.
coach_note: Committed movement beats weak ankle grabs — don't hesitate.

prompt: You have your opponent's legs stacked in a smash pass. They manage to get an underhook on your leg. What should you do?
options: Flatten them immediately and clear the underhook before it develops
Post on your hands and try to move forward anyway
Let go and return to standing
correct_answer: Flatten them immediately and clear the underhook before it develops
explanation: An underhook on your leg during a smash pass can let them reverse position or recover — flattening their hips and clearing the underhook immediately stops that from developing.
coach_note: An underhook means they're working a reversal — shut it down fast.

prompt: Your opponent is on their back with open guard and keeps extending one leg straight into your stomach every time you try to approach. What is the simplest solution?
options: Push their extended leg to the side and step around it
Grab the extended leg and pull it toward you
Duck under the extended leg
correct_answer: Push their extended leg to the side and step around it
explanation: A single extended leg blocking your approach is easily cleared by redirecting it sideways — this opens a path to pass without engaging the leg directly.
coach_note: Clear the obstacle and step around — keep it simple.

prompt: You're standing in open guard top and your opponent suddenly sits up and grabs your belt with both hands. What do you do?
options: Sprawl your weight back and flatten their grip before they can pull you into guard
Step forward into their guard to break their balance
Grab their head to prevent them from getting under you
correct_answer: Sprawl your weight back and flatten their grip before they can pull you into guard
explanation: A belt grab with a sit-up is a pulling sweep or re-guard setup — sprawling your weight back breaks the pull and keeps you from being dragged into their guard.
coach_note: Sit-up and belt grab = sweep attempt. Sprawl back immediately.

prompt: You're passing and have moved to the side, but your opponent gets their knee in and re-establishes half guard on your leg. What do you do?
options: Keep your weight heavy and work to free the trapped leg before they settle in
Back off and return to open guard
Jump to the other side
correct_answer: Keep your weight heavy and work to free the trapped leg before they settle in
explanation: Half guard is a recovery position — staying heavy and working to free the leg immediately prevents them from establishing a full half guard game.
coach_note: Don't stop when you hit half guard — it's a speed bump, not a wall.

prompt: You're on top in open guard and your opponent has both feet on your hips. They are looking to do a basic push sweep. What grip kills this sweep?
options: Grab both of their ankles or shins before they can push
Grab the mat for base
Grab your own collar to tighten your core
correct_answer: Grab both of their ankles or shins before they can push
explanation: Grabbing their legs prevents them from generating the push force needed to sweep you — it also gives you offensive options to pass.
coach_note: Control the feet that are on your hips — grips stop the sweep.

prompt: You're passing open guard and your weight is forward over your opponent. They punch both hands up under your armpits and begin to push you up. What's happening and what do you do?
options: They're framing to create space — lower your hips to flatten the frames
They're going for a choke — tuck your chin
They want a double underhook — let them have it
correct_answer: They're framing to create space — lower your hips to flatten the frames
explanation: Armpit frames push you up and away to recover guard — lowering your hips makes you heavier and flattens the frame, making it ineffective.
coach_note: Heavy hips kill frames — don't let them create that space.

prompt: You're circling to pass open guard on the right side. Your opponent posts their right hand on the mat and starts to roll away from you. What do you do?
options: Follow their roll and take their back as they turn away
Stop and reset back to the front
Try to hold them flat by grabbing their hips
correct_answer: Follow their roll and take their back as they turn away
explanation: When they turn away from you to recover guard, their back is temporarily exposed — following the roll and securing a back position is the best offensive option.
coach_note: When they give you their back, take it — don't chase the guard.

prompt: You're attempting a toreando and you have their legs pinned to one side. You're about to run around to pass, but you notice their near arm is free and reaching to grab you. What do you do?
options: Move faster to complete the pass before the arm can become a problem
Stop and clear the arm first
Switch to a different pass
correct_answer: Move faster to complete the pass before the arm can become a problem
explanation: At white belt level, a reaching arm doesn't pose an immediate threat — committing to the toreando with urgency lets you complete the pass before it becomes relevant.
coach_note: Speed is your answer when the legs are already controlled.

prompt: You're smashing through your opponent's open guard with your weight forward. Their knees are compressed and you're moving to side control. They grab your head with both hands. What do you do?
options: Keep your posture strong and continue driving your chest forward — don't let head grips stall you
Stop and peel their hands off your head before continuing
Straighten up to get your head free
correct_answer: Keep your posture strong and continue driving your chest forward — don't let head grips stall you
explanation: Head grips can stall a pass if you react by stopping or straightening up — driving through with posture and chest pressure makes the head grip irrelevant.
coach_note: Don't pause for head grips — keep driving forward.

prompt: You're on top passing and your opponent is framing on your knee with their foot to stop your knee slide. What do you do?
options: Lower your knee toward the mat and slide it over their foot
Pull your knee back and try a different angle
Push their foot away with your hand first
correct_answer: Lower your knee toward the mat and slide it over their foot
explanation: Their foot frame on your knee loses effectiveness when your knee drops to the mat — the slide continues with the leg flattened out.
coach_note: Knee down defeats the foot frame — mat contact wins.

prompt: You have your opponent stacked and are trying to pass. They suddenly get a grip on your sleeve and try to extend your arm to create space. What do you do?
options: Bring your elbow tight to your body and continue driving forward with your bodyweight
Pull your arm free before continuing the pass
Release the stack and stand up
correct_answer: Bring your elbow tight to your body and continue driving forward with your bodyweight
explanation: Keeping the elbow tight collapses the extension and removes their leverage — continuing forward with weight keeps the pressure on the stack.
coach_note: Elbow tight to the body kills sleeve-extension attempts.

prompt: You're in open guard top and want to do a toreando pass but your opponent has grips on both your sleeves. What do you do first?
options: Strip the sleeve grips before grabbing their ankles
Grab their ankles and ignore the sleeve grips
Back step to break the grips by creating distance
correct_answer: Strip the sleeve grips before grabbing their ankles
explanation: Sleeve grips prevent you from controlling their legs effectively for the toreando — breaking them first puts you in position to dominate the leg exchange.
coach_note: You can't do a toreando with no hands — strip grips first.

prompt: You're passing open guard and have moved to side control. Your opponent gets their knee in and frames on your neck to prevent you from settling. What do you do?
options: Lower your head and shoulder to collapse the neck frame, then work to flatten their hips
Back off and accept half guard
Reach for an Americana from this position
correct_answer: Lower your head and shoulder to collapse the neck frame, then work to flatten their hips
explanation: The neck frame creates space — collapsing it with head and shoulder pressure flattens your opponent and lets you complete the transition to side control.
coach_note: Head and shoulder pressure collapses the neck frame — stay tight.

prompt: Your opponent pulls your sleeve hard while pushing your collar to break your posture and sweep. Which is the most dangerous grip to break first?
options: The sleeve grip — it's the motor of the collar-sleeve attack
The collar grip — it's closer to your head
Break both at the same time
correct_answer: The sleeve grip — it's the motor of the collar-sleeve attack
explanation: The sleeve grip is what drives the collar-sleeve system by controlling your arm and breaking your structure — eliminating it shuts down their sweep mechanics.
coach_note: No sleeve grip = no collar-sleeve. Break the sleeve first.

prompt: You're standing over open guard and your opponent puts both hands on your knees and keeps blocking every time you try to step forward. What do you do?
options: Grab their wrists and remove their hands, then quickly step forward
Step sideways to change the angle and re-engage
Drop to your knees to get under their hands
correct_answer: Grab their wrists and remove their hands, then quickly step forward
explanation: Hand-on-knee frames are easily broken by grabbing the wrists and removing them — immediately following up with a step forward doesn't give them time to re-establish.
coach_note: Remove the hands, move fast — don't leave them time to re-grip.

prompt: You're attempting the leg weave smash pass. You have the weave locked in and are trying to drive your weight to the side. Your opponent gets onto their side facing you. What does this mean for your pass?
options: It's more difficult now — flatten them back down before continuing
It's easier now — go to side control immediately
Switch to a different pass since this one is done
correct_answer: It's more difficult now — flatten them back down before continuing
explanation: When they get onto their side during a smash pass, they've partially recovered and can create more resistance — you need to re-flatten them to re-engage the smash.
coach_note: Hips flat = smash works. On the side = you lost some control.

prompt: You're standing in open guard top and want to engage. Your opponent is on their back with knees up and feet ready. What is the safest way to step in to start passing?
options: Step in at an angle to the side rather than straight in between their legs
Step straight in between their knees quickly
Drop to your knees from outside their guard
correct_answer: Step in at an angle to the side rather than straight in between their legs
explanation: Stepping straight in between the legs puts you in the most dangerous position for sweeps and guard retention — angling in gives you a pass path with less risk.
coach_note: Never step straight into open guard — always angle your approach.
